title: Nine Hundred Dollars for Nothing
summary: The AWS bill jumps by nine hundred dollars a day. The workload hasn't changed, but something is quietly burning money.

resolution.root_cause: All S3 traffic from EC2 instances in the private subnet (10.0.2.0/24) routes through the NAT Gateway (nat-0a1b2c3d4e5f67890) because no S3 VPC gateway endpoint exists. The NAT Gateway charges $0.045 per GB of data processed. With approximately 500 GB of S3 uploads daily, the data processing charge alone is roughly $907 per day. The pipeline has been running for three months, accumulating approximately $27,000 in unnecessary NAT Gateway charges.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that NAT Gateway data processing charges are the source of the excessive cost
Player identifies that no S3 VPC gateway endpoint exists, causing all S3 traffic to route through the NAT Gateway
Player proposes creating an S3 VPC gateway endpoint and updating the route table
Player calculates the approximate cost savings from eliminating NAT Gateway data processing charges for S3 traffic

learning_objectives: VPC gateway endpoints for S3 route traffic directly to S3 within the AWS network at no additional cost
NAT Gateway charges $0.045 per GB of data processed, which adds up quickly with high-volume data pipelines
Private subnets without gateway endpoints route all traffic through the NAT Gateway, including traffic to AWS services
Route tables for gateway endpoints use prefix lists (pl-xxxxxxxx) to direct S3-bound traffic to the endpoint instead of the NAT Gateway
sop_steps: Turn on network traffic logs (called VPC flow logs) so you can see where data from your private servers is going. Look for which destinations are sending the most data through the NAT Gateway -- a relay service that charges you per gigabyte.
Open the AWS spending dashboard (Cost Explorer) and filter by NAT Gateway. Look at the daily charges to see how much you are paying and when the high charges started.

Look at the private subnet's route table -- this is the list of rules that tells your servers where to send traffic. Check whether any shortcuts to S3 or DynamoDB exist (these shortcuts are called VPC gateway endpoints). In this case, none exist.

Create a free shortcut so traffic to S3 stays inside the AWS network instead of going through the NAT Gateway. This shortcut is called an S3 VPC gateway endpoint. Use the command aws ec2 create-vpc-endpoint with --vpc-endpoint-type Gateway and attach it to the private subnet's route table.

After creating the endpoint, check the route table to confirm a new entry appeared. This entry uses a prefix list (a set of S3 IP addresses, written as pl-xxxxxxxx) and points to the new endpoint. Because this rule is more specific than the catch-all rule that sends everything through the NAT Gateway, S3 traffic now takes the free path instead.
Confirm your data pipeline still writes to S3 without errors. Gateway endpoints work invisibly -- your application code and AWS SDK calls do not need any changes.
Watch the NAT Gateway charges in Cost Explorer over the next 24 hours. You should see the daily cost drop sharply because S3 traffic no longer passes through it.
Check whether any servers in the private subnet also talk to DynamoDB (a managed database service). If so, create a DynamoDB gateway endpoint the same way -- it is also free.
Set up a spending alert (a CloudWatch billing alarm) that notifies you if NAT Gateway charges exceed a daily threshold. This catches similar problems in days instead of months.
Check all other private subnets in your network for servers that send large amounts of data to S3 or DynamoDB. Each subnet needs its own route table entry to use the gateway endpoint.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Gateway endpoint created but attached to the wrong route table
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The endpoint exists, but it was linked to the wrong route table -- for example, the public subnet's route table instead of the private subnet's. The private subnet still has no shortcut to S3, so traffic continues going through the NAT Gateway and you keep getting charged.
related_failure_modes.prevention: When creating the endpoint, list every private subnet route table that needs the S3 shortcut using the --route-table-ids option. Then verify each route table actually has the new prefix list entry by running aws ec2 describe-route-tables.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Accessing an S3 bucket in a different region
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: A gateway endpoint only works for S3 buckets in the same AWS region as your network (VPC). If your pipeline writes to a bucket in a different region, the traffic still goes through the NAT Gateway and you pay both the NAT Gateway fee and a cross-region data transfer fee.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Keep your S3 buckets in the same region as the servers that use them. If you must access buckets in another region, know that the gateway endpoint will not help, and plan your budget accordingly.

related_failure_modes.scenario: An endpoint security policy blocks your application
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: When creating a gateway endpoint, you can attach a policy that restricts which buckets or actions are allowed. If this policy is too restrictive, your pipeline's write requests (PutObject calls) get denied. Instead of saving money, the pipeline breaks.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Use the default policy (which allows all access) unless you have a specific security reason to restrict it. If you add a custom policy, test it against your application's actual S3 operations before applying it in production.

sop_practices: Whenever you build a private network (VPC) with private subnets, add S3 and DynamoDB gateway endpoints from the start. They cost nothing and prevent servers from routing AWS-service traffic through the NAT Gateway, which charges per gigabyte.
Set up spending alerts for NAT Gateway charges before you launch high-volume workloads. Catching a cost problem in the first week is much cheaper than discovering it three months later.

Use network traffic logs (VPC flow logs) alongside the spending dashboard (Cost Explorer) to figure out which traffic patterns are driving NAT Gateway costs. If the traffic is going to S3 or DynamoDB, a free gateway endpoint can eliminate the charge.
After creating a gateway endpoint, confirm the route table entry is in place and then check Cost Explorer over the next 24 hours to verify that charges dropped as expected.

glossary.NAT Gateway: A managed AWS service that lets instances in a private subnet send traffic to the internet. It charges per hour and per gigabyte of data it processes, in both directions.
glossary.VPC Gateway Endpoint: A free routing target you add to a route table so that traffic to S3 or DynamoDB stays inside the AWS network instead of going through a NAT Gateway or the internet.
glossary.Prefix List: An AWS-managed list of IP address ranges for a service like S3. When a gateway endpoint is created, a route using the prefix list is added to the route table automatically.
glossary.Private Subnet: A subnet with no direct route to the internet. Instances in it must use a NAT Gateway or VPC endpoint to reach services outside the VPC.
glossary.Route Table: A set of rules that determines where network traffic from a subnet is directed. Each entry maps a destination IP range to a target like a NAT Gateway, internet gateway, or VPC endpoint.
glossary.Data Processing Charge: The per-gigabyte fee that a NAT Gateway charges for every byte of traffic it handles. At $0.045 per GB, this cost grows quickly when large amounts of data pass through it.
glossary.Cost Explorer: An AWS tool that lets you view and filter your AWS spending by service, usage type, and time period. It is how most teams discover unexpected charges.
glossary.VPC Flow Logs: Records of network traffic going to and from network interfaces in a VPC. Each record shows source, destination, port, protocol, and byte count.

components.name: EC2 Pipeline Instances (10.0.2.14, .15, .16)
components.role: Three c5.2xlarge instances in the private subnet that ingest sensor data and write approximately 500 GB daily to S3
components.failure_impact: If instances stop, the data pipeline halts and sensor data is not ingested

components.name: Private Subnet (10.0.2.0/24)
components.role: Hosts the pipeline instances with no direct internet access, all outbound traffic routed through the NAT Gateway
components.failure_impact: If the route table has no path to S3, the pipeline cannot write data

components.name: NAT Gateway (nat-0a1b2c3d4e5f67890)
components.role: Provides outbound internet access for the private subnet. Currently handles all S3 traffic at $0.045 per GB
components.failure_impact: If the NAT Gateway fails, all outbound traffic from the private subnet stops, including S3 writes

components.name: Route Table (rtb-0d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1)
components.role: Directs all non-local traffic from the private subnet to the NAT Gateway via the 0.0.0.0/0 route
components.failure_impact: Without a prefix list route for S3, all S3 traffic is sent through the NAT Gateway and incurs data processing charges

components.name: S3 Bucket (fieldspar-sensor-lake-prod)
components.role: Stores sensor data from the pipeline, approximately 45 TB total, in the same region as the VPC
components.failure_impact: If the bucket is inaccessible, pipeline writes fail and sensor data is lost or queued

components.name: S3 VPC Gateway Endpoint (missing)
components.role: Would route S3 traffic directly from the private subnet to S3 without traversing the NAT Gateway, at no cost
components.failure_impact: Its absence is the root cause -- all S3 traffic takes the expensive NAT Gateway path

system.data_flow: Sensor data arrives at the three EC2 pipeline instances in private subnet 10.0.2.0/24. The instances use the AWS SDK to write objects to the S3 bucket fieldspar-sensor-lake-prod. The private subnet route table has only two routes: 10.0.0.0/16 to local and 0.0.0.0/0 to the NAT Gateway. S3 traffic matches the 0.0.0.0/0 route, passes through the NAT Gateway in the public subnet, then through the Internet Gateway, and arrives at S3. The NAT Gateway charges $0.045 per GB for every byte it processes.
system.what_broke: The VPC was built during a two-week sprint before the first customer demo. A NAT Gateway was created for outbound internet access, but no S3 VPC gateway endpoint was added. When the pipeline went live in January, all 500 GB of daily S3 traffic began routing through the NAT Gateway. The $0.045/GB data processing charge accumulated to $907 per day. Nobody examined line-item billing for three months. The total unnecessary spend reached $27,135.

progressive_clues: look at the bill breakdown -- which service has the highest data transfer charges?
how does traffic from a private subnet reach Amazon S3? check the route table for the private subnet
there is a way to route S3 traffic that does not go through the NAT Gateway -- look at VPC endpoints
an S3 VPC gateway endpoint routes traffic directly to S3 within the AWS network, it is free, and no gateway endpoint exists in this VPC
